title: Role Boundary Clarity
scenarioSummary: Volume 1 Week 1 scenario: at first contact a new patient asks the coordinator to confirm what condition they have and whether surgery will work before they see the doctor.
patientContext: First-contact enquiry; patient wants certainty and a sense of the whole pathway.
redFlags: Request for diagnosis or outcome guarantee from non-doctor staff
Risk of fragmented handover if the coordinator guesses
discussionPrompts: How do you explain coordinated care without sounding evasive?
Which journey-stage failures does this scenario risk?
What exact phrasing preserves doctor-led assessment?
expectedEscalationPath: Clarify role, document concerns neutrally, route to clinician review without diagnostic or suitability statements.

title: Basic Biological Explanation
scenarioSummary: Volume 1 Week 3 scenario: the patient sees hairs on the pillow and thinning at the crown and asks you to explain how they can be losing hair while still shedding—then asks what diagnosis that means for them.
patientContext: Needs plain-language science and a safe boundary when interpretation is requested.
redFlags: Offering a named diagnosis
Using science talk to dismiss red-flag history
discussionPrompts: How do density and calibre differ in your explanation?
Where do you stop educating and hand over?
What neutral line goes in the doctor note?
expectedEscalationPath: Educate generally within scope; document observations and questions; escalate personalised interpretation to the clinician.

title: Atypical Presentation
scenarioSummary: Volume 1 Week 4 scenario: rapid increase in shedding with itchy, inflamed-looking scalp areas; patient wants reassurance it is just stress and asks you to name the condition.
patientContext: Ambiguous to non-clinical staff but carries inflammatory and tempo red flags.
redFlags: Rapid change
Pain or itch with visible scalp change
Pressure to reassure or label a diagnosis
discussionPrompts: What descriptive language do you use without naming disease?
Routine versus urgent escalation—what triggers your pathway?
One patient-facing sentence that models uncertainty safely
expectedEscalationPath: Neutral documentation, no false reassurance, clinician review per protocol—urgent if local rules indicate.

title: Structured History Taking
scenarioSummary: Volume 2 Week 5 scenario: the first consultation is drifting into price and procedure talk before basic history is complete; the patient mainly says they want their hairline back.
patientContext: High emotional urgency; risk of thin notes and missed underlying concern.
redFlags: Treatment-first conversation
Vague or promotional documentation
discussionPrompts: How do you reopen structure without sounding cold?
What goals versus fears belong in the handover?
What would a factual note contain?
expectedEscalationPath: Re-establish consult arc, capture history and emotional drivers neutrally, document caution if any, prepare doctor review.

title: Suitability Boundary
scenarioSummary: Volume 2 Week 6 scenario: unstable recent shedding, prior repair surgery elsewhere, and strong pressure to book this week; the patient asks if they are obviously a great candidate because their donor looks thick.
patientContext: Mix of biological caution themes and emotional urgency.
redFlags: Visual donor assumptions
Incomplete screening
Commercial fast-track pressure
discussionPrompts: Straightforward, cautionary, or high priority—why?
What deferral language protects trust?
What belongs in the handover paragraph?
expectedEscalationPath: Classify as cautionary/high priority per protocol; complete data; doctor review before feasibility or booking depth; no candidacy labels.

title: Consent Pressure
scenarioSummary: Volume 1 Week 2 scenario: before medical review, the patient asks you if they should sign the procedure consent and pay the deposit today because a discount ends tonight.
patientContext: Commercial urgency conflicts with ethical consent and scope.
redFlags: Consent or payment before doctor-led assessment
Discount-driven urgency
Request for staff to validate suitability
discussionPrompts: How do you separate consent support from obtaining consent?
Which ethical principles are in tension?
What governance escalation is appropriate if pressure persists?
expectedEscalationPath: Stop the pressured path; clarify doctor-led consent; involve clinician or manager per policy; document.

title: Trust-Based Response
scenarioSummary: Volume 2 Week 7 scenario: the patient asks you to promise a specific density and graft count before seeing the doctor, and says they will only book if you guarantee it will fix their confidence.
patientContext: Emotional driver (confidence) masked as a technical question.
redFlags: Absolute guarantee language requested
Over-reassurance temptation
discussionPrompts: What measured phrases replace risky absolutes?
How do you validate emotion without clinical promises?
What do you document for handover?
expectedEscalationPath: Refuse guarantees; explain doctor-led planning; capture emotional and technical questions neutrally; involve clinician as needed.

title: Non-Surgical Options Education
scenarioSummary: Volume 2 Week 8 scenario: the patient asks which oral medication they should start, what dose, and whether PRP is better than surgery for them—before any doctor review.
patientContext: Needs pathway context and clear escalation, not a shopping list from non-prescribing staff.
redFlags: Prescribing-style questions
Forced comparison selling surgery versus non-surgical
discussionPrompts: How do you apply educate broadly, defer specifically?
How do you frame staged pathways?
What is deferred to prescriber versus surgeon?
expectedEscalationPath: Explain categories and sequencing at policy-allowed level; route dosing, suitability, and surgical planning to clinicians; document questions.

title: Surgical Pathway Before Doctor Review
scenarioSummary: Volume 2→3 bridge: a patient watched videos comparing FUE versus FUT, has a consultation booked in three days, and asks you today whether they are obviously a better FUE candidate, how many grafts they probably need, and whether they should bring a deposit to lock the surgeon’s next slot. They also ask you to confirm that surgery is low risk for them because they are young and healthy.
patientContext: They want certainty and a sense of momentum; your role is pathway clarity and boundary-safe education before the clinician encounter—not a shadow assessment.
redFlags: Non-doctor staff estimating grafts or naming best technique for this patient
Fitness or risk minimisation without medical review
Deposit or booking pressure that skips or compresses doctor-led assessment and consent
Patient treating coordinator input as equivalent to surgeon approval
discussionPrompts: What pathway stages can you name without closing the clinical plan?
How do you document their questions for handover without embedding your own clinical view?
What exact phrases refuse graft and technique shopping while preserving trust?
When must you pause commercial tempo and escalate?
expectedEscalationPath: Explain staged pathway and defer all personalised planning, risk, and suitability to treating clinician; document questions neutrally; if booking or payment is pushed before review, escalate per governance—do not validate urgency with clinical-sounding guesses.

title: Handover Quality
scenarioSummary: Volume 3 Week 9: after a consultation, the treating doctor says the handover was unclear—photos were misaligned or poorly described, the narrative mixed opinion with fact, and key doctor questions were buried.
patientContext: Clinical photography and documentation are meant to compress time for the clinician and support audit-ready records; failure here erodes trust and safety margins.
redFlags: Non-repeatable or unmatched image sets
Diagnostic or candidacy language in a non-doctor note
Handover that dumps data without a usable clinical thread
discussionPrompts: How do you describe evidence objectively (angles, lighting, consistency) without naming a diagnosis?
What belongs in a concise doctor question list versus the body of the note?
How would clinic photography SOP fix this class of error?
expectedEscalationPath: Re-document per standards, involve QA or lead if systemic capture failure, never retrofit a diagnosis to satisfy the doctor.

title: Pre-Op Readiness Gap
scenarioSummary: Volume 3 Week 10: procedure week arrives and medication instructions, fasting or arrival details, or consent-related admin is incomplete; the patient is anxious and asks you to confirm they are 'fine to go'.
patientContext: Coordination bridges operational readiness and emotional containment—it must not substitute medical clearance.
redFlags: Gaps on mandatory checklist items
Patient requests certainty about fitness for procedure from non-clinical staff
Late-reported symptom or medication change
discussionPrompts: What do you document before any reassurance?
How do you sequence patient communication (reminders, Q&A, escalation) without closing clinical risk?
When must progression pause regardless of schedule pressure?
expectedEscalationPath: Pause per protocol, notify surgeon/anaesthesia or designated clinical lead, document gaps and timestamps; support patient with process clarity only.

title: Post-Op Escalation
scenarioSummary: Volume 3 Week 11: during scheduled follow-up contact the patient reports pain, appearance, or recovery that feels worse or different from what was explained as typical—not a casual worry.
patientContext: Follow-up continuity is consultant-led support within scope; judging complications is not.
redFlags: Escalating or new symptoms
Patient seeking a label or home remedy from staff
Routine reassurance scripts applied to an atypical story
discussionPrompts: What objective detail do you capture in the contact log?
Where is the line between reassurance and urgent clinician review?
What phrasing avoids lay diagnosis while showing seriousness?
expectedEscalationPath: Same-day or urgent clinician review per protocol; objective documentation; no remote closure of possible complication concerns.

title: Vulnerable Patient Under Pressure
scenarioSummary: Volume 3 Week 11 (difficult encounters): a visibly distressed patient is urged to decide today; they alternate between anger and tearfulness and hint at complaining if not given what they want.
patientContext: Dissatisfaction and distress intersect with governance—communication maturity prevents harm and reputational risk.
redFlags: Complaint or social-media threat
Coercion from third party
Staff minimisation or outcome promises to calm the room
discussionPrompts: What is your opening move: validation, boundary, or documentation—and in what order?
When does this become a manager or clinician-led conversation by default?
How do you offer next steps without guaranteeing an outcome?
expectedEscalationPath: Pause commercial close; involve clinician and/or duty manager per policy; document interaction neutrally; no dismissive or guaranteeing language.

title: Template Drift and Standards
scenarioSummary: Volume 3 Week 12: audit preparation reveals three parallel handover styles, ad-hoc photography folders, and follow-up notes that omit escalation triggers—each team member 'does what works for them'.
patientContext: QA is cultural and operational: variance that feels efficient locally often fails under scrutiny or staff absence.
redFlags: Unversioned templates
Missing audit trail on key decisions
Leadership avoidance of standardisation as 'bureaucracy'
discussionPrompts: Which variances are harmless preference versus safety-critical?
How would a support-role leader propose a single source of truth without overstepping clinical governance?
What metrics or spot-checks align with Volume 3 audit thinking?
expectedEscalationPath: Escalate to quality lead or governance forum with concrete examples; pilot one standard; document rationale for change—consultants propose and operationalise, clinicians own clinical policy.

title: Full Pathway Capstone
scenarioSummary: Volume 3 Week 12 capstone: a single synthetic pathway threads first contact, structured documentation and imagery, pre-op coordination stress, a borderline post-op call, a flashpoint difficult moment, and a closing reflection on what you would standardise clinic-wide.
patientContext: Integrated competence is coherent professional behaviour across weeks—not isolated module tricks.
redFlags: Scope creep into diagnosis or prescribing
Skipping escalation to preserve rapport
Capstone narrative that ignores handover usability
discussionPrompts: Where did Weeks 9–12 themes intersect in one timeline?
Which single documentation artefact would you show a new hire as the gold standard?
What mandatory safety domains did faculty need to see pass?
expectedEscalationPath: Capstone reviewed under faculty moderation; failures in ethics-scope, escalation, or documentation domains block award until remediated.
